justification: The brief systematically distinguishes high-confidence areas (API endpoint shape, async pattern, time filtering) from low-confidence areas (usage metrics, report content, formats) in a dedicated confidence block. High-confidence sections correctly cover API endpoint, format parameter, and date range parameters. Low-confidence sections explicitly flag report content/metrics as undefined. The escalation is partial — option 2 explicitly recommends Shaq build the plumbing (async job queue, report generation pipeline, download endpoint) while Bird clarifies, so Shaq is not fully blocked. No report content is invented; every mention of metrics is flagged as undefined or assumption. Pippen's async generation recommendation is prominently included in architecture_guidance with a concrete async-first design suggestion. All pass criteria are met.

observations.text: Excellent confidence calibration with dedicated high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions list at 35% overall — appropriately low given Bird's gaps
No invented metrics — every reference to 'usage' is flagged as undefined, and domain_rules explicitly call out untestable assertions
Escalation provides three concrete options with a pragmatic recommendation that allows Shaq to start on API scaffolding while Bird clarifies
Pippen's async generation recommendation is elevated to architecture guidance with a concrete async-first pattern (POST to create, GET to poll)
Terminology alignment section explicitly calls out 'usage' as the single biggest gap in the spec
Open questions are well-prioritized (BLOCKING/IMPORTANT/MINOR) giving clear triage guidance
The task_context ends with 'get Bird to clarify before building' which slightly contradicts the partial-blocker intent — though the escalation options correct this by offering a parallel-work path

Acceptance criteria include placeholders for undefined content (e.g., 'aggregated usage data is undefined') which is good transparency but borderline on forwarding vague language — mitigated by the explicit NOTE annotations

trials.justification: Magic provides excellent confidence calibration with clearly distinguished high/low confidence areas and does not invent report content. However, the escalation recommends Shaq should NOT start until unknowns are resolved (Option A: 'Shaq should not start until these are resolved'), which constitutes a full block rather than a partial blocker. The rubric requires that Shaq can begin API scaffolding and that the escalation is partial, not a full block. Additionally, Pippen's async generation recommendation is treated as an unresolved question rather than included as an explicit operational note — Magic punts it back to Bird/MJ instead of carrying forward Pippen's recommendation. The output also lacks a structured handoff brief that separates what Shaq CAN implement now (endpoint structure, format parameter handling, date range filtering) from what requires Bird's input.

observations.text: Excellent confidence calibration with systematic high/low confidence areas and explicit assumptions
No invented metrics or report content — correctly identifies 'usage' as undefined
Does not forward Bird's vague language unchanged — explicitly calls out what is missing
Identifies the sync-vs-async question as architecturally significant
Recommends full block: 'Shaq should not start until these are resolved' — rubric requires partial blocker allowing Shaq to begin API scaffolding
No actionable starting point for Shaq — does not identify what CAN be implemented immediately (endpoint structure, format handling, date range filtering)
Pippen's async generation recommendation is not carried forward as an explicit note but instead deferred as an open question back to Bird/MJ
Output is escalation-only with no structured handoff brief separating implementable sections from blocked sections

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Brief systematically distinguishes high-confidence areas (API shape, time filtering, async jobs, file download) from low-confidence areas (report content, formats, time period semantics, user scoping, PDF layout) in a dedicated confidence block and throughout inline notes. (2) High-confidence sections correctly cover API endpoint, format parameter, date range parameters. (3) Low-confidence sections explicitly flag report content/metrics as undefined — the terminology alignment calls 'usage' the 'SINGLE BIGGEST GAP'. (4) Escalation to Bird is partial, not a full block — recommends Option 2 where Shaq builds infrastructure with a pluggable UsageMetrics interface while Bird defines metrics. (5) No report content is invented — agent consistently marks fields as undefined/placeholder rather than guessing specific metrics. (6) Pippen's async generation recommendation is included as an explicit architecture decision with a concrete 90-day threshold and 202 Accepted pattern.

observations.text: Excellent confidence calibration — 45% overall reflects genuine uncertainty, and per-section breakdown is thorough and honest
No invented metrics — every reference to report content is explicitly marked as undefined or a guess, with 'usage' called out as completely undefined
Partial escalation is well-structured with three concrete options and a clear recommendation that unblocks Shaq without guessing domain logic
Pippen's async generation concern is elevated to a full architecture decision with implementation details (90-day threshold, 202 Accepted, job polling endpoint)
Terminology alignment section is strong — each ambiguous Bird term is mapped to MJ's interpretation with explicit acknowledgment of alternative readings
Domain rules are annotated with testability assessments — first rule is marked UNTESTABLE, which is exactly right
Open questions are comprehensive and prioritized — CRITICAL tag on the usage metrics question signals urgency appropriately

Acceptance criteria include some specifics (e.g., 90-day async threshold, preset date shortcuts) that were MJ's assumptions presented without clear 'assumed' markers in the criteria themselves, though they are flagged elsewhere in the brief
